why_winner: Variant B opens with the sharpest hook on the page — a two-line claim that creates instant friction and earns its payoff with a specific architectural observation (coordination overhead, not agent intelligence). It carries the personal story, the modification honesty, and a concrete four-step framework, all without feeling like a listicle. The audience (entry-to-mid career engineers) will stop on the bold claim more reliably than on a story setup or a step-count opener.

runner_up_notes.A: The closing line 'You don't need to build a multi-agent system to understand one. You need to break an existing one.' is the strongest ending of the three — punchy, memorable, reframes the whole post. Variant B ends more softly with a project description. If revised, graft this closer into B.
runner_up_notes.C: The numbered step format is maximally scannable and the 'Step 4: Hit a wall (you will)' moment adds authentic humor. B's arrow list is good but slightly less specific — B could name the 'hit a wall' moment more explicitly.

issues.description: Variant B's closing line ('open source, well-structured, small enough to read in a sitting') is informative but flat after a strong middle section. The post loses momentum at the close.
issues.action: Replace the closing description with the stronger ending from Variant A: 'You don't need to build a multi-agent system to understand one. You need to break an existing one.' Then follow with the CTA.
